audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 14, first-pass restored draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Jonathan's initiative and armor-bearer power dynamics, battlefield sign, ethnic language, textual ark/ephod issue, panic and defections, Saul's food oath, honey, blood-eating, lot procedure, death sentence, popular ransom, war summaries, family names, and anti-oath/child-soldier safeguards.

verses.restored: One day Jonathan son of Saul said to young man carrying his armor, Come, let us cross to Philistine outpost on other side. But he did not tell his father.
Saul was staying at edge of Gibeah under pomegranate tree at Migron, and people with him were about six hundred.
Ahijah son of Ahitub, brother of Ichabod, son of Phinehas, son of Eli, priest of YHWH at Shiloh, was carrying ephod. People did not know Jonathan had gone.
Between passes by which Jonathan sought to cross to Philistine outpost was a tooth of rock on one side and a tooth of rock on other; name of one was Bozez and name of other Seneh.
One tooth stood north before Michmash, and other south before Geba.
Jonathan said to young man carrying his armor, Come, let us cross to outpost of these uncircumcised. Perhaps YHWH will act for us, for nothing restrains YHWH from saving by many or by few.
His armor-bearer said to him, Do all that is in your heart. Turn yourself; look, I am with you according to your heart.
Jonathan said, Look, we are crossing to men and will reveal ourselves to them.
If they say thus to us, Wait until we reach you, then we will stand in our place and not go up to them.
But if they say thus, Come up to us, then we will go up, for YHWH has given them into our hand. This will be sign for us.
Both revealed themselves to Philistine outpost. Philistines said, Look, Hebrews are coming out of holes where they hid.

Men of outpost answered Jonathan and his armor-bearer and said, Come up to us, and we will make a thing known to you. Jonathan said to armor-bearer, Come up after me, for YHWH has given them into hand of Israel.
Jonathan climbed upon his hands and feet, and armor-bearer after him. They fell before Jonathan, and armor-bearer put them to death after him.
That first striking which Jonathan and armor-bearer struck was about twenty men within about half a furrow of a yoke of land.
Trembling was in camp, field, and all people. Outpost and destroyers also trembled; earth quaked, and it became a trembling of Divine.
Saul's watchmen in Gibeah of Benjamin saw, and look, multitude was melting and going here and there.
Saul said to people with him, Muster now and see who has gone from us. They mustered, and look, Jonathan and his armor-bearer were not there.
Saul said to Ahijah, Bring near ark of Divine—for ark of Divine was at that time with children of Israel, according to Hebrew text.
While Saul spoke to priest, tumult in Philistine camp went on and increased. Saul said to priest, Withdraw your hand.
Saul and all people with him were called together and came to battle. Look, every Philistine sword was against companion, a very great confusion.
Hebrews who had been with Philistines before, who went up with them into camp, also turned to be with Israel who were with Saul and Jonathan.
All men of Israel who had hidden in hill country of Ephraim heard Philistines fled, and they also clung after them in battle.
YHWH saved Israel that day, and battle passed beyond Beth-aven.

Men of Israel were hard pressed that day, for Saul put people under oath, saying, Cursed is man who eats bread before evening and until I have avenged myself on my enemies. So none of people tasted bread.
All land entered forest, and there was honey upon ground.
When people entered forest, look, flow of honey; but no one brought hand to mouth, for people feared oath.
Jonathan had not heard when his father made people swear. He extended tip of staff in his hand, dipped it in honeycomb, brought his hand to mouth, and his eyes brightened.
A man from people answered and said, Your father strictly made people swear, saying, Cursed is man who eats bread today. And people were faint.
Jonathan said, My father has troubled land. See, please, that my eyes brightened because I tasted a little of this honey.
How much more if people had eaten freely today from spoil of enemies they found; for now striking among Philistines has not been great.
They struck Philistines that day from Michmash to Aijalon, and people were very faint.
People pounced upon spoil, took sheep, cattle, and calves, slaughtered them upon ground, and people ate with blood.
They told Saul, saying, Look, people are sinning against YHWH by eating with blood. He said, You have acted faithlessly. Roll a great stone to me today.

Saul said, Disperse among people and say to them, Bring near to me each his ox and each his sheep, slaughter here, and eat; do not sin against YHWH by eating with blood. All people brought near each what was in his hand that night and slaughtered there.
Saul built an altar to YHWH; it was first altar he built to YHWH.

Saul said, Let us go down after Philistines by night and plunder them until morning light, and let us not leave a man among them. They said, Do all good in your eyes. Priest said, Let us draw near here to Divine.
Saul inquired of Divine, Shall I go down after Philistines? Will you give them into hand of Israel? But he did not answer him that day.
Saul said, Come near here, all corners/chiefs of people, and know and see in what this sin has been today.
For as YHWH lives, who saves Israel, even if it is in Jonathan my son, he shall surely die. But none among all people answered him.
He said to all Israel, You shall be on one side, and I and Jonathan my son will be on other side. People said to Saul, Do what is good in your eyes.
Saul said to YHWH Divine of Israel, Give completeness/truth. Jonathan and Saul were taken, and people went out.
Saul said, Cast between me and Jonathan my son. Jonathan was taken.
Saul said to Jonathan, Tell me what you have done. Jonathan told him and said, Tasting, I tasted a little honey with tip of staff in my hand. Look, I must die.
Saul said, Thus may Divine do and thus add, for dying you shall die, Jonathan.

People said to Saul, Shall Jonathan die, who made this great salvation in Israel? Far be it! As YHWH lives, not one hair of his head shall fall to ground, for with Divine he worked this day. So people ransomed Jonathan, and he did not die.
Saul went up from pursuing Philistines, and Philistines went to their place.
Saul took kingship over Israel and fought around against all his enemies: Moab, children of Ammon, Edom, kings of Zobah, and Philistines. Wherever he turned, he inflicted punishment/defeat.
He acted valiantly, struck Amalek, and rescued Israel from hand of its plunderers.
Sons of Saul were Jonathan, Ishvi, and Malchi-shua. Names of his two daughters: name of firstborn Merab and name of younger Michal.
Name of Saul's wife was Ahinoam daughter of Ahimaaz. Name of commander of his army was Abner son of Ner, uncle of Saul.
Kish father of Saul and Ner father of Abner were sons of Abiel.
War was severe against Philistines all days of Saul. Whenever Saul saw any mighty man or any valiant man, he gathered him to himself.

translation_notes: Jonathan initiates a two-person attack without informing Saul. Courage and tactical creativity coexist with grave risk to himself and his armor-bearer.
The armor-bearer's agreement occurs within military hierarchy; it must not be romanticized as unrestricted consent to lethal danger.
'Uncircumcised' is ancient boundary rhetoric and may not become an ethnic or religious slur.
Jonathan's sign is a battlefield interpretation, not a safe general method for discerning Divine approval.
Earthquake, panic, defections, hidden people rejoining, and intra-Philistine killing complicate any simple heroic account.
Saul's oath centers 'my vengeance' and deprives exhausted troops of food. It is reckless leadership and spiritual coercion.
Jonathan's honey restores him physically. Nourishment is not moral weakness, and denying food does not prove devotion.
The people's later blood-eating follows extreme hunger created by the oath; leaders share responsibility for predictable harm caused by deprivation.
Saul moves quickly from an unknown offense to a death oath against even his son. Public vows do not override life, proportionality, due process, or parental duty.
The people collectively ransom Jonathan and stop the execution. Resisting an abusive command can be faithful communal action.
Verse 36's proposal to leave no man alive is exterminatory battle speech and cannot be a model.
The royal summary normalizes continual warfare and recruitment of strong men; it should be read under chapter 8's conscription warning.
Saul's daughters and wife are named but not reduced to dynasty assets; later narratives require attention to their agency.
safety_note: Reject reckless secret missions, ethnic slurs, food-denial oaths, spiritualized exhaustion, summary execution, child sacrifice by parental vow, exterminatory speech, and leader claims that personal vengeance is national salvation. The people's rescue of Jonathan shows that communities may and should stop a ruler's lethal command.
